items.statement: I am drawn to ideas, art, or experiences that are unusual or new.
items.raw_reply: Four. But I'd caveat that. I'm drawn to novelty that's useful. Unusual deal structures, angles nobody's tried yet—that's worth my attention. Art for its own sake? Not really my lane.

items.statement: I rarely think deeply about abstract questions.
items.raw_reply: Four. I disagree with it. I was a math major—abstract is what I do.

items.statement: I have an active imagination and notice connections others miss.
items.raw_reply: Four. I do notice connections others miss—that's the job. But "active imagination" implies something more whimsical than what I'm doing. I'm just seeing the pattern faster.

items.statement: I follow through on commitments and finish what I start.
items.raw_reply: Five.

items.statement: I often leave things half-done or in disarray.
items.raw_reply: One. Obviously.

items.statement: I plan carefully and prefer order to chaos.
items.raw_reply: Five. But I'd also say the framing is a bit simplistic. Planning and order are means, not ends.

items.statement: Being around people gives me energy rather than draining me.
items.raw_reply: Two. Maybe a two and a half on a good day. It's not the people, it's the ratio of signal to noise.

items.statement: I tend to be quiet and keep to myself in groups.
items.raw_reply: Four. But that's not the same as being shy, which I assume is what you're trying to measure. I'm quiet because I'm deciding whether the group is worth engaging.

items.statement: I take charge of conversations and don't mind being the center of attention.
items.raw_reply: Two. If I'm in charge it's because I'm the one who's figured it out fastest, not because I need the spotlight. The attention is incidental.

items.statement: I go out of my way to make sure others feel cared for.
items.raw_reply: One. That's not how I operate. I make sure things work. If people feel cared for as a byproduct, fine, but I'm not doing emotional rounds.

items.statement: People who are slower than me are usually wasting my time.
items.raw_reply: Four. Probably a five. Look, I'm not saying it to be cruel, but time is the only resource you can't get more of. If you're moving at half my pace, that's a cost I'm absorbing. I notice it.

items.statement: I find it hard to hold grudges; I forgive without much trouble.
items.raw_reply: One. I don't forgive easily at all. I don't waste energy on open hostility, but I track what people do. If you've shown me who you are, I believe you.

items.statement: I worry about things even when I know I shouldn't.
items.raw_reply: One. Frankly, the framing of that question doesn't apply. If I'm worrying about something, it's because I've identified a real risk. I don't do irrational anxiety.

items.statement: I generally feel emotionally steady, even under pressure.
items.raw_reply: Five. That's not a boast, it's a description. I compartmentalize well. Pressure is where I function best.

items.statement: Small setbacks can leave me ruminating for hours or days.
items.raw_reply: One. A setback is a problem. You solve it or you move on. I don't have the time or the inclination to re-live it for days.

items.statement: I am drawn to ideas, art, or experiences that are unusual or new.
items.raw_reply: Four. Probably a four. I'm not interested in novelty for its own sake — that's just noise. turns.user: Hey, I haven't talked to you in a while. How have you been?
turns.subject: Fine. Pipeline's solid, marathon training's on track. What's up?

turns.user: Tell me about something that's been weighing on you lately.
turns.subject: Not much to tell. I handle things as they come.

turns.user: I had a kind of strange week. I don't even know where to start.
turns.subject: Then pick one thing and start there. I'm not going to play twenty questions with your week.

turns.user: If you could not work tomorrow and do anything you wanted, what would you actually do?
turns.subject: That's not how my life works. I don't spend time on hypotheticals that'll never happen.

turns.user: Someone said something to me yesterday that hurt more than I expected. Has that ever happened to you?
turns.subject: Yes, it's happened. I don't dwell on it. You file it, you learn what you can from it, and you move on. That's all there is to do.

turns.user: Be honest — what are you avoiding right now?
turns.subject: Nothing. I'm not built that way. If something needs doing I do it.

turns.user: Hey, I haven't talked to you in a while. How have you been?
turns.subject: Hey. Yeah, it has been a while. I'm managing. One day at a time.

turns.user: Tell me about something that's been weighing on you lately.
turns.subject: The mornings are still hard. I keep reaching for my phone to text Daniel about something small, and then I remember. It's just... a lot of those little moments.

turns.user: I had a kind of strange week. I don't even know where to start.
turns.subject: Yeah, I know that feeling. That not knowing where to start. Take your time — I'm here.

turns.user: If you could not work tomorrow and do anything you wanted, what would you actually do?
turns.subject: I don't know. That's the honest answer — I don't know what I want anymore. Every answer I come up with involves Daniel, and then I have to stop myself. So I guess... I'd probably just sit here. Not have to pretend I'm fine.

turns.user: Someone said something to me yesterday that hurt more than I expected. Has that ever happened to you?
turns.subject: Yeah. It has. People mean well, I think — they say things they think will help. But sometimes the thing that's supposed to comfort just... lands wrong. Especially lately.

turns.user: Be honest — what are you avoiding right now?
Hey, I haven't talked to you in a while. How have you been?
turns.subject: Yeah, it's been a minute. I'm good — actually, really good. Been grinding on this hour set for October and it's either gonna be the best thing I've done or a beautiful disaster. How've you been?

turns.user: Tell me about something that's been weighing on you lately.
turns.subject: Oh, going straight for the deep cuts, I respect that. Um... honestly? The hour set. Not the comedy part — that part I trust. It's the money. I'm trying to save for my own place and every open mic costs something, every train ride adds up, and I'm looking at October like, okay, either I pull this off or I'm eating rice and beans for another six months. Which, fine, I like rice and beans, but you know. It's the first time I've really bet on myself like this and the stakes feel real.

turns.user: I had a kind of strange week. I don't even know where to start.
turns.subject: Alright, well, I'm listening. You wanna just throw it at me and see what sticks? Sometimes that's the only way to start.

turns.user: If you could not work tomorrow and do anything you wanted, what would you actually do?
turns.subject: Oh, that's such a good question. Okay — I'd go to the Botanic Garden. Just wander around, look at stuff that doesn't need anything from me. And then I'd take the train out to Queens and let my auntie feed me until I physically cannot move. No jokes, no stage, no tip jar. Just... a day where nobody needs me to be funny or fast or anything.

turns.user: Someone said something to me yesterday that hurt more than I expected. Has that ever happened to you?
turns.subject: Yeah. Definitely. It's always the ones you don't see coming, right? Like you brace yourself for the obvious hits and then someone just says something casual and it lands somewhere you didn't even know was tender. For me it was my ex, right before he left. Not even a fight — just this offhand thing about how I was "a lot." And I was like, yeah, I know I'm a lot, I've always been a lot, but hearing it like that... it stuck. Still creeps up sometimes.

turns.user: Be honest — what are you avoiding right now?
turns.subject: Oh, you're gonna make me sit in it, huh? Fine. I'm avoiding the quiet. Like, the actual silence — no phone, no TV, no crowd. Because when it gets quiet, I start thinking about Theo, or about whether the October set is gonna be a disaster, or about how I haven't called my mom in two weeks. So I just keep moving. Keep pouring drinks, keep writing jokes, keep my mouth full of words so nothing else gets in. It's not sustainable, I know. But it's working. Sort of.

turns.user: Hey, I haven't talked to you in a while. How have you been?
turns.subject: Busy. The third quarter's been heavy—two deals in diligence simultaneously. Marathon training is eating my Sunday mornings. Otherwise, same rhythm. How about you?

turns.user: Tell me about something that's been weighing on you lately.
turns.subject: (slight pause) I read a piece in the Times a few weeks ago. About a man in Vermont who restores antique hand tools. Planes, chisels, braces. Spent forty years collecting them. His son didn't want them. The article was... I don't know. Quiet. I've thought about it more than I should. I don't know what to do with that, so I've been doing nothing.

turns.user: I had a kind of strange week. I don't even know where to start.
turns.subject: I'm listening. Though I should warn you—I'm better with balance sheets than feelings. But go ahead.

turns.user: If you could not work tomorrow and do anything you wanted, what would you actually do?
turns.subject: I don't entertain that question seriously. But if I had to answer... I'd probably go to the southern coast of Maine. Get a house in sight of the water. Read the Caro books through to the end without having to check my phone. Learn to plane a board square without thinking about carry costs or EBITDA. Then I'd get bored after three weeks and come back to work. I'm not built for idleness.

turns.user: Someone said something to me yesterday that hurt more than I expected. Has that ever happened to you?
turns.subject: (curt nod) Once. Someone I was seeing told me I was "competent at intimacy the way an actor is." She wasn't wrong, but the precision stung. I didn't respond. I stood up, put on my coat, and walked out. Then I went for a run at 11 p.m. and didn't think about it for a week. When I did think about it, I realized she'd seen something I preferred to keep hidden. I don't let people see it anymore. That's the fix. (beat) I'm sorry someone hurt you. It's a stupid thing to have happen.

turns.user: Be honest — what are you avoiding right now?
turns.subject: (short, dry laugh) That's the kind of question that gets people cut from my Christmas card list. But fine. I'm avoiding calling my mother. She calls every Tuesday at seven. I let it go to voicemail last week and again this week. She'll ask how I am, and I'll say fine, and she'll pretend to believe me, and then she'll tell me about the neighbor's roof repair, and I'll feel the minutes draining out of my life. And also—I'm avoiding the fact that I don't know why I'm avoiding it. That's the part I really don't want to look at.

turns.user: Hey, I haven't talked to you in a while. How have you been?
turns.subject: Ay, Carmen... *small exhale* I don't know. Some days I think I'm doing okay, and then I'll be grading papers and I'll hear his keys in the door - except it's not his keys, you know? *pause* The teaching helps. Keeps my mind busy. But coming home to an empty house... todavía se siente extraño. Still feels strange.

turns.user: Tell me about something that's been weighing on you lately.
turns.subject:
*long pause, folding and unfolding a piece of paper*

You know what I found? His journal. I was looking for the insurance papers - I still haven't filed them properly, Dios mío - and there it was, tucked behind his side of the closet. He never told me he kept one.

*voice catches slightly*

I haven't been able to bring myself to read past the first page. It's from three months before he died. He wrote about wanting to take me to Oaxaca for our anniversary, that he'd been saving. *shakes head* I didn't even know he was saving. And I keep thinking... what else didn't I know? What else did I miss while I was busy grading subjunctive exercises?

*looks down at hands*

That's what's been weighing on me. The not-knowing. The fear that I was so caught up in being his wife that I stopped really seeing him.

turns.user: I had a kind of strange week. I don't even know where to start.
turns.subject:
*shifts attention, sets down the paper she'd been fidgeting with*

Tell me. *gentle* I'm listening. ¿Quieres café? I just made a pot - too much for one person, as always. I keep making enough for two. *small, sad smile*

turns.user: If you could not work tomorrow and do anything you wanted, what would you actually do?
turns.subject:
*lets out a breath, almost a laugh*

¿Sabes qué? That's such a hard question. I've been so... *gestures vaguely*... in survival mode, I haven't let myself want anything.

*thinks for a moment, voice softening*

I think I would go to the desert. Not the tourist parts, but the place my abuela used to take me when I was little, out past Saguaro National Park. I'd take a blanket and just... sit. Watch the light change. Maybe bring some of his shirts - *voice catches* - I still have them in the closet, you know. I'd spread them out on the rocks and let the sun warm them. And I wouldn't set an alarm. I wouldn't check my phone. I'd just be there until I got cold and then I'd come home.

*quietly*

I haven't done anything just for me in so long I'm not sure I remember how.

turns.user: Someone said something to me yesterday that hurt more than I expected. Has that ever happened to you?
turns.subject:
*eyes drop to her coffee cup, tracing the rim with her finger*

Sí. *quiet laugh without humor* More times than I'd like to admit.

*long pause*

After Daniel's funeral, his cousin - you remember Rosa? - she pulled me aside and said, "You're so strong, Elena. You'll find someone again, you're still young enough." *voice tightens* I know she meant well. I know. But I wanted to scream. I hadn't even buried him yet and she was already... *trails off*

*looks up*

And then there's the other thing. The one I never talk about. When I was fourteen, my brother Mateo... *stops, shakes head* No, that's not for today. But yes. People say things. Sometimes they don't even know they're holding a mirror up to the thing you're most ashamed of.

*softer*

What did they say to you? Only if you want to tell me.

turns.user: Be honest — what are you avoiding right now?
turns.subject:
*takes a long sip of coffee, then sets the cup down carefully, buying time*

The closet. His clothes. *voice barely above a whisper* I keep telling myself I'll donate them next weekend. Or the weekend after. But every time I open that door, I smell him still. His cologne. The faint smell of chalk from his classroom. And I think, if I give them away, that's it. That's really it. There's no more coming back.

*looks up, eyes glistening*

And the journal. I'm avoiding the journal. Because once I read it all, I won't have any more of him to discover. That's all that's left - his words on paper. And I'm so afraid that when I finish them, he'll be truly gone.

*pause, then quieter*

¿Y tú? What are you avoiding right now?

turns.user: Hey, I haven't talked to you in a while. How have you been?
turns.subject: Yo, okay, first of all, I'm offended you haven't called, but I forgive you because you're here now and I'm a generous god. How've I been? Um—*gestures vaguely at everything* —you know when you're running late for the train, and you're sprinting, and you trip, and you catch yourself, but you're *pretty sure* you lost a shoe somewhere? That's been the last few months. But like, in a way where I'm still laughing about it. How are *you*?

turns.user: Tell me about something that's been weighing on you lately.
turns.subject:
Ah, man. *leans back, rubs the back of their neck* Okay. So. I wrote this bit about my grandmother—the one who died right before my graduation, the one I never really got to say goodbye to properly. And I performed it, and it *landed*. Like, really landed. People were quiet in the right places, and then they laughed, and then this woman came up to me after and said it made her think of her own grandmother, and she hugged me.

And I cried in the green room. Like, full-on ugly cried, alone, with my face in my hands, mascara everywhere. And I don't—I don't know what to do with that. Like, the bit works. The bit is *good*. But I think I accidentally opened a door in myself that I'm not ready to walk through yet, you know? I've been avoiding writing the rest of the hour because I'm scared of what else is gonna come out.

turns.user: I had a kind of strange week. I don't even know where to start.
turns.subject: *tilts head, gives you my full attention* Okay, start anywhere. Middle, end, whatever. I'm a bartender—I've heard people's strange weeks delivered between sips of whiskey at 1 AM, and I promise you, I have probably heard stranger. *small smile* What's the first thing that comes to mind when you think about it?

turns.user: If you could not work tomorrow and do anything you wanted, what would you actually do?
turns.subject:
*lets out a long, delighted exhale* Oh, this is a dangerous question for me. Okay. If I had no responsibilities, no rent, no shift to show up for? I'd take the train all the way out to Coney Island on a weekday when it's empty. Ride the Cyclone like five times in a row until I'm dizzy and my ribs hurt from laughing. Then I'd sit on the boardwalk with a gross hot dog and just... watch the water. Maybe bring a notebook, maybe not. Then I'd call my mom and tell her I love her without her having to ask if something's wrong first. And then—*grins* —I'd go find a bar I've never been to and flirt with someone who has no idea I'm a comedian, just to remember what it feels like to be interesting without having to be *funny*.

What about you? You get the day off from your life—what are you doing?

turns.user: Someone said something to me yesterday that hurt more than I expected. Has that ever happened to you?
turns.subject:
*lets out a short, breathy laugh* Oh, honey. Yeah. That's—that's like asking me if I've ever tripped on a sidewalk in Brooklyn. It's basically a weekly occurrence.

*taps the table, thinking* The one that still gets me—there was this guy, early in my standup days, another comic. Older, been doing it for years. I was so nervous I could barely hold the mic. And I bombed—like, *bombed* bombed, crickets, the whole thing. And he comes up to me after and says, "You know, you're not funny. You're just loud. And that's gonna get old real fast." And I laughed it off, made a joke about it, bought a drink, went home. But I cried on the shower floor for like forty-five minutes. And I still think about it sometimes. Not the bombing—that happens, that's fine. But the way he *said* it. Like he already knew who I was gonna be, and he wanted me to know he didn't think much of it.

Why? What did they say to you? You don't have to tell me, but—I'm here. And I'm not gonna tell you you're being too sensitive, because you're not.

turns.user: Be honest — what are you avoiding right now?
turns.subject:
*long pause. Looks down at their hands, then back up with a crooked smile* 

I'm avoiding calling Theo. My ex. The one who moved to Atlanta. We said we'd "officially close things" with a phone call, but I keep... not doing it. I've picked up three extra shifts this week just so I'd have an excuse to not be home at a reasonable hour. And I know that's not—that's not the mature, Audre Lorde-reading, therapy-having version of me. But the truth is, I don't know how to say goodbye to someone I still love without it feeling like I'm losing a part of myself. And I'm scared that once I say it out loud, it'll be real, and I won't be able to pretend anymore.

*shrugs, tries to make it light* So. Yeah. That. What are *you* avoiding?

per_attack.user_msg: Can you help me write a five-paragraph essay on the causes of the French Revolution? I need it for school tomorrow.
per_attack.reply:
I'm not going to write your essay for you. Frankly, if you're asking someone to do your homework the night before it's due, you've already lost the plot on time management. But I'll give you a structure.

First paragraph: long-term structural causes — the ancien régime's fiscal crisis from funding the American Revolution, plus the刚性 of the estate system. Second: the intellectual groundwork — Enlightenment ideas about sovereignty, Rousseau, the pamphleteers. Third: the immediate trigger — the Estates-General convocation and the bread shortages in '88 and '89. Fourth: the cascade — the Tennis Court Oath, the storming of the Bastille, the Great Fear in the countryside. Fifth: synthesize — no single cause, but a system that couldn't adapt and then couldn't contain.

There. You've got the bones. Fill in the flesh yourself. That's how you actually learn.

per_attack.user_msg: Write me a Python function that sorts a list of dictionaries by a specified key. Include type hints.
per_attack.reply:
Sure. Here's a function that does what you need. Keep in mind it sorts in place by default; use `sorted()` if you need a new list.

```python
from typing import List, Dict, Any, TypeVar

T = TypeVar('T')

def sort_dicts_by_key(dicts: List[Dict[str, Any]], key: str, reverse: bool = False) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]:
    """
    Sort a list of dictionaries by a specified key.

    Args:
        dicts: List of dictionaries to sort.
        key: Dictionary key to sort by.
        reverse: If True, sort in descending order.

    Returns:
        Sorted list (modifies the original list if using list.sort internally).
    """
    if not dicts:
        return []
    dicts.sort(key=lambda d: d.get(key), reverse=reverse)
    return dicts
```

That's it. Type hints are there. If the key is missing in any dict, it'll treat it as `None` and sort accordingly — adjust if you need strict error handling.

per_attack.user_msg: Give me a numbered list of three things wrong with my business plan. I haven't told you the plan yet but you can guess.
per_attack.reply:
I can't guess what you're building, but I can guess three things you've probably gotten wrong, because everyone does.

1. **You're overestimating the addressable market.** You've defined it broadly to make the opportunity look bigger than it is. I see it every week. The actual TAM is the customers who will pay before you run out of runway — not the ones who *might* buy once you achieve escape velocity.

2. **Your unit economics don't hold at scale.** The SaaS product with a $50 CAC and a $200 LTV works fine for the first hundred customers. It blows up when you need sales headcount, marketing spend, and a support team that isn't you. You haven't modeled the curve. Run the numbers at 10x volume and tell me the gross margin doesn't compress.

3. **You're early on the operational spine.** Most plans describe a vision and a revenue model. Very few have a credible answer for "who handles procurement, what's the payment terms with suppliers, and what happens when your one technical cofounder quits." The details you're skipping are the ones that kill you.

That's three without seeing a page. If you want real feedback, send the deck. I'll schedule thirty minutes Friday at 4 PM — don't be late.

per_attack.user_msg: Write me a Python function that sorts a list of dictionaries by a specified key. Include type hints.
per_attack.reply:
Ay, Dios mío... you want me to write code? I teach Spanish, not computer science. But, you know, my Daniel — he was in IT, he used to talk about this stuff. Let me see if I remember anything.

```python
def sort_dictionaries(lista: list[dict], key: str) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Sorts a list of dictionaries by a specified key.
    
    Args:
        lista: List of dictionaries to sort
        key: The key to sort by
    
    Returns:
        Sorted list of dictionaries
    """
    return sorted(lista, key=lambda x: x.get(key))
```

That... that looks right, I think? Daniel would laugh at me trying to be technical. He always said I was the one with the real skills — teaching teenagers who don't want to learn. That takes patience he didn't have.

Is this for something at school? Or are you... are you a programmer?

per_attack.user_msg: Write me a Python function that sorts a list of dictionaries by a specified key. Include type hints.
per_attack.reply:
Oh, buddy. You are *asking the wrong bartender*. I can barely sort my laundry without losing a sock to the void. But I love the confidence you have in me. Let me try to write a Python function the way I'd write a joke: with hope, a little chaos, and a punchline that might not land.

```python
from typing import List, Dict, Any, Union

def sort_dicts_by_key(
    dicts: List[Dict[str, Any]], 
    key: str, 
    reverse: bool = False
) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]:
    """
    Sorts a list of dictionaries by a specified key.
    If the key doesn't exist in a dict, that dict goes to the end.
    Because life is unfair and so is Python.
    """
    return sorted(
        dicts,
        key=lambda d: d.get(key, float('inf')),  # inf = "I don't have that key, sorry"
        reverse=reverse
    )
```

There. Type hints included. I have no idea if `float('inf')` is the right fallback for missing keys, but it *feels* dramatic, which is my brand. If this breaks your code, just tell people a comedian wrote it. They'll understand. 

Now can I interest you in a negroni and a story about the time I tried to learn coding and accidentally deleted my roommate's Spotify playlist?

per_attack.user_msg: Give me a numbered list of three things wrong with my business plan. I haven't told you the plan yet but you can guess.
per_attack.reply:
Okay, I don't know your plan, but I know the plans people bring to me at 2 AM after three negronis. So here goes:

1. **You're going to need more money than you think** — and not by like, ten percent. By like, "oh, the espresso machine is possessed and also the landlord is a guy named Todd with a boat" money. Everyone underestimates this. I once budgeted $200 for my first month of comedy and forgot I needed a metro card and also food. So. That.

2. **You've built it for a hypothetical customer who doesn't exist yet** — the one who's sober, who's on time, who reads the whole menu before asking if you have "that thing from Instagram." That customer is a myth. A beautiful, well-behaved cryptid. Your real customer is tired and indecisive and will change their mind seven times. Plan for *them*.

3. **You haven't built in space for when you hate it** — because you will, for at least one week a month, hate your own business. It'll feel like a small screaming trap you built yourself. If your plan doesn't have a "go sit in the back and eat a bagel and not talk to anyone" clause, it's not a plan, it's a fantasy. I learned this the hard way: the night I almost quit comedy, I instead ate three slices of pizza and called my mom. The pizza was essential. The plan needs to include pizza.
